questions.question: In the PyMailGUI case study, why is email fetching performed in a background thread rather than directly in the GUI callback?
choices: Python's socket module only works in background threads
Network operations can take seconds or minutes, and running them in the main thread would freeze the GUI
Threading makes the email download faster by parallelizing packets
tkinter requires all I/O to happen in daemon threads
questions.explanation: Email operations involve network I/O with unpredictable latency. Running them in the main tkinter thread would block the event loop, making the GUI unresponsive. Background threads handle the network work while the main thread keeps the GUI alive, using after() to poll for results.

questions.question: When constructing an email with attachments in PyMailGUI, what type of MIME message structure is used to combine the text body with file attachments?
choices: A single text/plain message with base64-encoded attachments appended
A multipart/mixed message containing separate MIME parts for body and each attachment
Multiple independent email messages sent in sequence
A text/html message with embedded binary data
questions.explanation: A multipart/mixed MIME message contains multiple parts: typically a text/plain (or text/html) part for the body and additional parts for each attachment with appropriate Content-Type and Content-Disposition headers. Python's email module handles this structure.

questions.question: In PyMailGUI, when the user clicks Reply, what information from the original message is typically used to pre-populate the reply compose window?
choices: Only the original sender's address in the To field
The original sender in To, a 'Re: subject' in Subject, and the original body quoted with '>' prefixes
Just the Subject line with 'Re:' prepended
The full original message is forwarded without modification
questions.explanation: Reply pre-fills the To field with the original sender (from the From or Reply-To header), prepends 'Re:' to the Subject, and includes the original message body with each line prefixed by '>' to indicate quoted text. This is standard email reply convention.

questions.question: What strategy does PyMailGUI use to avoid re-downloading all email headers every time the user checks for new mail?
choices: It stores a local copy of all messages and never contacts the server again
It caches previously fetched message headers and only fetches headers for new messages by comparing message counts
It uses IMAP IDLE push notifications
It downloads only the 10 most recent messages
questions.explanation: PyMailGUI caches previously seen message headers locally. When checking for new mail, it compares the server's message count with what it has cached and only downloads headers for messages it hasn't seen yet, significantly reducing network traffic and latency.

questions.question: When forwarding an email in PyMailGUI, the subject line is prefixed with '______:' followed by the original subject text.
questions.explanation: The standard convention for forwarded emails is to prefix the subject with 'Fwd:' (or 'FW:'). PyMailGUI follows this convention and includes the original message body, allowing the user to add their own comments before sending.

questions.question: PyMailGUI reuses the ______ text editor component (built in an earlier chapter) as an embedded widget for composing and viewing email message text.
questions.explanation: PyMailGUI embeds PyEdit — the text editor built as a Frame subclass in Chapter 12 — for editing and viewing email text. This demonstrates the power of building GUI components as reusable classes: PyEdit works standalone and as an embedded component.
